chapters.link-speed: Sharing a link, not the folder
chapters.cast-step-1: Step 2: Both repos side by side

events.text: Now the fork. One command will clone the entire repo, the app, the database, the config files, regardless of size. One gigabyte, one hundred gigabytes, one terabyte: the size simply does not matter.
events.afterText: Fork done. The clone is up and running, regardless of size.
events.prose: A single command clones the entire repo: app, database, and config files. Fork time is constant regardless of repo size, whether one gigabyte, one hundred gigabytes, or one terabyte.

events.text: Both repos exist side by side now. Same machine, same disk, two completely independent environments.
events.afterText: Two repos, one disk. Same data, separate everything else.
events.prose: Both repos now coexist on the same machine and disk as two fully independent environments.

events.text: Let us prove the isolation. In the original, our marker file is right where we left it. The fork existence did not change anything in the parent.
events.afterText: Original file still there. The fork didn't touch it.
events.prose: Verify isolation by inspecting the original repo: the marker file remains in place. Creating a fork does not modify the parent.

events.text: Now let us delete that file, but only inside the fork. We are making a destructive change here.
events.afterText: Gone in the fork. The original stays untouched.
events.prose: Delete the marker file inside the fork only. This is a destructive change scoped to the fork.

events.text: Back to the original. The marker file is still here, untouched. Same image, separate Docker daemons, separate filesystems.
events.afterText: Still here. Forks are properly isolated.
events.prose: Switch back to the original repo: the marker file remains intact. The parent and fork share the same image but run on separate Docker daemons and separate filesystems.

events.text: When you are done, just delete the fork. The original stays exactly as it was. Fork, experiment, break things, delete. No risk.
events.afterText: Fork removed. The original is exactly as we left it.
events.prose: Delete the fork when finished. The original repo is unaffected, enabling a safe fork, experiment, and discard workflow.

narrations.text: Welcome to the killer feature! Clone an entire production repo regardless of size, and break nothing.
One parent repo fans out into as many independent clones as you want. Each fork is its own isolated environment running side by side with the original.
Think of sharing a folder link. The link is the same lightweight thing whether the folder is small or huge, and forking works on the same principle.
One gigabyte, one hundred gigabytes, one terabyte: the fork behaves the same. Repo size does not factor into the equation.
The parent repo is the sun and your fork is a mirror catching its light. The shared base stays untouched while anything you paint on the mirror belongs only to you.
A fork is a frozen photograph of the parent at one instant. The parent keeps flowing like a river, but your snapshot stays exactly where it was.
Five forks of a hundred gigabyte repo still add up to about a hundred gigabytes on disk. You only pay for the bytes you actually change inside each fork.
Forks deliberately leave secrets behind, which is what makes clone production, break nothing actually safe. We wire those up properly in the next tutorial!
